title_en: Bryan Johnson Is Going to Die
title_zh: 布莱恩·约翰逊注定会死

paragraphs.en: At first, the two bowls of fruit on Bryan Johnson’s kitchen island look perfect. They’re brimming with plump kiwis, hardy avocados, and ripened bananas. These are the food of the gods, I figure, for a man who aspires to live like one. But then I look closer. A lone orange, its skin flecked with mold, sits adjacent to two lemons, both almost entirely consumed by a layer of white fuzz. Something, it seems, is rotten in the estate of Johnson.
paragraphs.zh: 起初，布莱恩·约翰逊厨房岛上摆着的两个水果碗看起来很完美：里面装满了鼓鼓的猕猴桃、结实的牛油果和熟透的香蕉。按照我的理解，这就是“神明之食”，很符合一个想按神一般方式生活的男人的口味。可我再看仔细些，就发现了不对劲。一只橙子，表皮上长满了霉斑，旁边放着两颗柠檬，几乎被一层白色绒毛覆盖得差不多全白掉了。总觉得在约翰逊的庄园里，某个地方已经开始腐烂。

paragraphs.en: The line between science and wellness has been blurred beyond recognition. WIRED is here to help.
paragraphs.zh: 科学与养生之间的界线已经被模糊得不复存在。连线媒体 WIRED 来帮你理清。

paragraphs.en: That estate, it’s worth noting, is a predictable one. Johnson’s home in Venice, California, is the angular, concrete-floored template of a dwelling you’d assume is owned by a man. Specifically a man who worked in tech, made his millions, and subsequently embarked on a midlife, post-wealth search for purpose. All of which Johnson is, and did, and still appears to be doing: After selling his web payments company, Braintree, for $800 million in 2013, Johnson parted ways with both his wife and his lifelong Mormon religion. In 2021 he announced Project Blueprint, an effort designed to reverse his own body’s aging process. This involves an all-consuming, unproven regimen including but not limited to daily exercises, blood tests, a doctrinal sleep routine, MRIs, plasma transfers, scalp stimulants, urine tests, several dozen supplements, Dexa scans, light therapy, and caloric restriction.
paragraphs.zh: 值得注意的是，这座“庄园”并不出人意料。约翰逊在加州威尼斯的住所，是一栋棱角分明、混凝土地面的居所模型，像极了你会想象中由一个男人拥有的住宅。准确地说，是一个在科技圈干过并赚了大钱、随后在中年离开财富后又开始寻找人生意义的人所拥有的房子。而约翰逊正是如此，而且他曾经是、也似乎仍在这样做：2013 年，他以 8 亿美元出售了自己的网络支付公司 Braintree 后，离开了妻子，也脱离了自己信仰了毕生的摩门教会。2021 年，他宣布了“Project Blueprint”——旨在逆转自己身体衰老过程的计划。该计划包含且不限于每日锻炼、抽血化验、严格的睡眠仪式、核磁共振成像、血浆输注、头皮刺激、尿液检测、数十种补充剂、DEXA 扫描、光疗和热量限制等一整套耗时且未经验证的方案。

paragraphs.en: If the rotting fruit didn’t give it away, then no, this is not a kitchen where household memories are made over milk and cookies—although a collage of candid photos taped above Johnson’s stove offers hints of familial warmth. (Johnson has three kids, one of whom is infamous for donating data on his youthful erections and his own plasma to his father’s anti-aging efforts.) This is a kitchen, after all, that shares a home with specimen cups of semen and coolers of Johnson’s blood; where pills and powders, which I find meticulously stocked in Johnson’s walk-in pantry, are mixed, optimized, and consumed; where food is not eaten so much as nutrition is performed.
paragraphs.zh: 如果腐烂的水果没有先行揭示这一点，那么可以说，这里并不是一个靠牛奶和曲奇饼制造家庭回忆的厨房——尽管约翰逊炉灶上方贴着的生活照拼贴，确实透露出一点家庭温度。（约翰逊有三个孩子，其中一个甚至以向父亲的抗衰老项目捐献自己青春期勃起数据和血浆而闻名。）这毕竟是一间与精液样本杯和装满约翰逊血液的保温箱共享厨房空间的地方；这里的药片和粉剂我在约翰逊的步入式储藏间里看得很齐全，它们被调配、优化、吞服；这里的“食物”不是吃进去，而是被当作营养来执行。

paragraphs.en: Performance, of course, is Johnson’s specialty. There’s the performance of his body, which Johnson claims is now the single healthiest on Earth. And there’s how that body shows up to the viewing public, which it does quite often. Johnson has been the subject of dozens of profiles and interviews, as well as a Netflix documentary released earlier this year. He has amassed more than 4 million followers across YouTube, Instagram, and X, and he posts an ongoing stream of content about his sleep habits (sublime), his diet (meticulous), and his erections (trigger warning). Johnson has also used his online reach to push back against recent controversies, including a legal battle with his former fiancée Taryn Southern, and a New York Times investigation into his extensive use of confidentiality agreements to prevent, among other things, Blueprint employees from publicly talking about Johnson as well as his business dealings.
paragraphs.zh: 约翰逊当然擅长“表演”。先说他的身体状态，他自己宣称这是地球上目前最健康的身体；再说他在公众面前呈现这个身体的方式，他又经常这样做。约翰逊曾是数十篇专题报道和采访的对象，今年早些时候还有一部 Netflix 纪录片发布。通过 YouTube、Instagram 和 X，他已经积累了超过 400 万粉丝，并持续发布关于自己睡眠习惯（超乎想象）、饮食（谨慎到近乎挑剔）以及勃起状况（触发警示）的内容。约翰逊还借助自己的网络影响力对抗最近的争议，包括与前未婚妻塔琳·萨瑟兰（Taryn Southern）的法律纠纷，以及《纽约时报》对他大规模使用保密协议进行调查；而这些协议据称被用来阻止包括 Blueprint 员工在内的相关人员公开谈论约翰逊以及他的商业交易。

paragraphs.en: Over a 90-minute conversation, Johnson spoke at length about his longevity protocol, his assessment of RFK Jr.’s MAHA movement, and those agreements that he continues to enforce. He also took great pains to convince me—and all of you—that this wasn’t just about health and longevity. No, like most tech men living in boxy modernist homes and saddled with illusions of grandeur, Johnson has a new holy grail with which to galvanize his faithful following: artificial intelligence, baby.
paragraphs.zh: 在长达 90 分钟的谈话中，约翰逊详细讲述了他的长寿方案、他对 RFK Jr. 的 MAHA 运动的判断，以及那些他仍在执行的协议。他还费尽心思说服我，也说服在场的你们，告诉大家这不仅仅是健康与长寿问题。并非如此。与大多数住在方正现代主义建筑里、又背负着崇高幻象的科技男士一样，约翰逊也有一个新的“圣杯”，用来凝聚他忠实追随者的新信仰：人工智能，宝贝。

paragraphs.en: This interview has been edited for length and clarity.
paragraphs.zh: 这次采访经过了删节和澄清。

paragraphs.en: KATIE DRUMMOND: I’m going to ask you a very simple true-or-false question that you can answer however you want. Ready?
paragraphs.zh: 凯蒂·德拉蒙德：我想问你一个非常简单的真假问题，你可以按自己的方式回答。准备好了吗？

paragraphs.en: True or false: You, Bryan Johnson, the man sitting across from me, one day at some point in the future, will die.
paragraphs.zh: 对错问题：你，布莱恩·约翰逊，未来某一天——在未来的某个时刻——会死去。

paragraphs.en: Death has always been inevitable, so we have made all these preparations. We talk about immortality in professional achievements. We talk about life after death. There are the ways that we’ve dealt with death up to this point. And now we have this real possibility of extending our lifespans to some unknown horizon. So that’s extension. But we also have the ability to begin moving ourselves to computational systems. So currently, in a very crude form, I have a Bryan AI that has digested everything I’ve ever said.
paragraphs.zh: 死亡一直是不可避免的，所以我们做了所有这些准备。我们在职业成就中讨论永生。我们讨论死后的生活。到目前为止，这些都是我们处理死亡的方式。现在我们有了这一真实可能：把寿命延长到某个未知的地平线。那就是延寿。但我们也拥有开始将自己转移到计算系统中的能力。所以如今，我已经有了一个非常粗糙的“布莱恩AI”，它已经吸收了我说过的全部内容。

paragraphs.en: You do currently have this?
paragraphs.zh: 你现在真有这个吗？

paragraphs.en: And that Bryan AI is pretty good. As the technology gets better and better, the most prized asset is going to be existence; immortality as we thought about it before, through accomplishment or through offspring or the afterlife, will be devalued relative to existing. And that’s my fundamental bet on the future.
paragraphs.zh: 这个布莱恩AI已经相当不错了。随着技术越来越先进，最珍贵的资产将会是“存在”；在此之前我们所理解的永生——无论是通过成就、后代，还是来世——相较于“存在”来说都会被贬值。这就是我对未来的根本赌注。

paragraphs.en: If there was a world where, let’s say in five years, you could upload Bryan into an AI—and AI Bryan is pretty much as good as Bryan Bryan—does Bryan Bryan eat the cheeseburger?
paragraphs.zh: 如果有这么一个世界，比如说五年后，你可以把布赖恩上传到一个人工智能里——而这个AI版布赖恩几乎和布赖恩本人一样好——那么这个布赖恩会去吃那份奶酪汉堡吗？

paragraphs.en: Let’s think about your question in a different way. Most people today spend every waking moment pursuing wealth; and the time they’re not spending pursuing wealth, they’re pursuing some sort of status or prestige. When you give birth to superintelligence, you can start extending lifespans to some unknown horizon: 200 years, 1,000 years, 10,000 years. Millions of years. We don’t know. When that happens, the entire game of humanity shifts from that singular focus on wealth accumulation and status and prestige to existence. Now, embedded in existence, we may still play games of power, but it will be conditioned that existence itself is the highest virtue. That’s the shift that’s starting to happen right now.
paragraphs.zh: 我们换个角度看你的问题。今天，大多数人把清醒的每一刻都拿来追逐财富；而他们不在追逐财富时，就在追逐某种身份或声望。当你创造出超智能时，你就可以把寿命延长到一个未知的边界：200年、1000年、1万年，甚至几百万年。我们不知道会是多少。发生这种变化时，人类游戏的重心就会从单一追求财富、地位和声望，转向“存在”本身。如今，在“存在”之中，我们可能仍然会玩权力博弈，但它会被前提化：存在本身是最高的价值。这就是如今正开始发生的转变。

paragraphs.en: Let’s move the conversation to your immediate existence. You’ve talked at length about your protocol. Walk us through a day in the life right now.
paragraphs.zh: 我们把话题转回你当前的生命状态。你已经长篇大论地谈过你的方案。能不能带我们走一遍你现在的日常？

paragraphs.en: OK, cool. I have built my entire existence around sleep. My sleep profile is that of an early twentysomething. I’ve worked very hard at this. So it’s eight hours and 34 minutes. I’m up less than one time per night on average, I go to bed within two to three minutes of my head hitting the pillow, and I have 94 percent sleep efficiency. Typically, most people’s sleep signal is like the stock market, and mine is just flat.
paragraphs.zh: 好，当然。我的整个存在都围绕着睡眠来构建。我的睡眠状态几乎像一个二十来岁的年轻人一样。我为此非常用心地努力过。我的睡眠时间是8小时34分钟。平均每晚我醒来一次以下，躺下后两到三分钟就入睡，并且我的睡眠效率是94%。通常，大多数人的睡眠曲线像股市一样起伏不定，而我的却几乎是平稳的。

paragraphs.en: But to do that, you can’t just show up and say, “I’m going to put my head on the pillow and fall asleep.” You have to build your whole life system around that. So my day begins the night before when I go to sleep, and then I wake up around 4:30 or 5:00. First thing when I wake up, I get out of bed. I try to get up within one minute of waking.
paragraphs.zh: 但要做到这一点，你不能只是出现并说："我要把头枕在枕头上就去睡觉。" 你必须围绕它建立起完整的生活系统。所以我的一天要从前一晚开始，当我去睡觉时也已经开始了，然后我大约在凌晨4:30到5:00之间醒来。醒来后第一件事就是下床。我尽量在醒来后一两分钟内起身。

paragraphs.en: I try to avoid the “10 more minutes” or pull the phone up and start scrolling. I get up, I will get light in my eyes within a few minutes of waking—I wake up before the sun, so it’s 10,000 lux light. I’ll take my inner ear temperature. One thing we’ve noticed, as I’ve done this for the past four years, my body temperature has dropped almost 4 degrees Fahrenheit. And there’s good evidence that species with lower basal temperatures live longer.
paragraphs.zh: 我尽量避免说"再躺10分钟"，也不拿起手机开始刷屏。我起身后，会在醒来后几分钟内让眼睛接触光线——我在日出前醒来，所以有大约10,000勒克斯的光照。我会测量耳内体温。我们注意到，在过去四年这样做后，我的体温已经下降了近4华氏度。并且有充分的证据表明，基础体温较低的物种寿命更长。

paragraphs.en: I will then put a serum on my hair, my scalp. Rub my scalp with a silicone scrubber for hair growth. I’ll take a quick shower, and then I’ll come downstairs. I have a morning drink. I will eat something, I’ll work out for an hour. I’ll do red light therapy, then hyperbaric oxygen therapy, then some sauna, then I’ll rinse off, and then get ready for work.
paragraphs.zh: 接着我会在头发和头皮上涂一层精华液，用硅胶刷头皮进行刺激以促进头发生长。然后我快速淋浴，接着下楼。我会喝一杯早晨饮品。我会吃些东西，然后锻炼一小时。之后做红光疗法，再做高压氧疗法，再做一些桑拿，然后冲洗，接着开始准备上班。

paragraphs.en: That is quite a regimen. And then you stop eating for the day at …?
paragraphs.zh: 这套作息非常严格。然后你要在……时候停止全天进食？

paragraphs.en: Really quickly, rapid fire, ask me a couple questions about myself, and then tell me how I’m doing in the Bryan Johnson universe.
paragraphs.zh: 很快地、像连珠炮一样先问我几个关于我自己的问题，然后告诉我在布莱恩·约翰逊的宇宙里我表现如何。

paragraphs.en: What is your resting heart rate?
paragraphs.zh: 你的静息心率是多少？

paragraphs.en: 48 beats per minute.
paragraphs.zh: 每分钟 48 次。

paragraphs.en: What is your most recent body inflammation blood test result, your hsCRP?
paragraphs.zh: 你最近的血液炎症指标，hsCRP，检查结果是多少？

paragraphs.en: Bryan, I have no idea.
paragraphs.zh: 布莱恩，我完全不知道。

paragraphs.en: What is your blood glucose level?
paragraphs.zh: 你的血糖值是多少？

paragraphs.en: OK. How many continuous push-ups can you do?
paragraphs.zh: 好吧，你能连续做多少个俯卧撑？

paragraphs.en: OK. If you stood on one foot and closed your eyes, how long could you stay standing?
paragraphs.zh: 好吧，如果你只站在一只脚上并闭上眼睛，你能站多久？

paragraphs.en: Yeah. Try me. Not right now.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，来试试吧，但现在不行。

paragraphs.en: What is the length of your telomeres?
paragraphs.zh: 你的端粒长度是多少？

paragraphs.en: OK. What are your omega levels?
paragraphs.zh: 好吧，你的Omega水平是多少？

paragraphs.en: These are very involved questions.
paragraphs.zh: 这些问题都非常复杂。

paragraphs.en: What is your speed of aging?
paragraphs.zh: 你的衰老速度是多少？

paragraphs.en: Well, according to the skin test I took earlier, Bryan, it was 1.9.
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，按照我前阵子做的皮肤测试，Bryan，你是 1.9。

paragraphs.en: I thought you were just going to ask me if I smoke. Or exercise. Or sleep enough.
paragraphs.zh: 我还以为你只是要问我是不是抽烟、锻炼，或者睡得是否充足。

paragraphs.en: I can pose a question, like “How’s your sleep?” And you can give me a generic answer like “I sleep great,” but if you look at the actual data of your telomere length, it tells me the story of your overall health. These are readouts of your biology. They just say, “This is me in raw form.” There’s no storytelling. It’s just the data.
paragraphs.zh: 我可以问你一个问题，比如“你睡得怎么样？”然后你会给我一个泛泛的回答，比如“我睡得很好”，但如果你看看你端粒长度的实际数据，它会告诉我你整体健康的真实情况。这些是你生物学的读数。它们只是说：这是我的原始状态。它们只是数据。

paragraphs.en: I’m relieved that I don’t have that data for you right now. Now, you were a successful entrepreneur. You felt like you weren’t living your healthiest life. You parted ways with Mormonism. You got divorced. You went through all of these seismic life changes. A lot of people make major changes to their lives. Exceedingly few people—very, very, very, very, very few people—go as far as you have. What made you take this to such an extreme?
paragraphs.zh: 我很高兴你现在还拿不到这些数据。你当时是位成功的创业者。你觉得自己并没有过上最健康的生活。你和摩门教分道扬镳。你离过婚。你经历了这些巨大的生命转折。很多人都会对自己的人生做出重大改变。极少数人——非常、非常、非常、非常非常少数的人——会走得像你这么远。是什么让你把这件事推进到这么极端？

paragraphs.en: I’m really motivated by having read various biographies of people throughout time and place. In their moment, they were able to identify the most far-ranging ambition identifiable in that moment. You couldn’t have sequenced the genome in the year 1800. You probably couldn’t have gone to space in the 1920s. In any given time, a new emergent possibility is present. Then two questions arise. One is: What is it? And two is: Will somebody do it?
paragraphs.zh: 我真正的动力来源于读过各个时代、各个地方的人的传记。在他们所处的那个时刻，他们都能识别出那个时代里最具突破性的、最宏远的目标。你不可能在1800年就对基因组进行测序。你也大概不可能在1920年代就飞向太空。在任何时代，总会有一种新出现的可能性。然后就会出现两个问题。一个是：那是什么？另一个是：会有人去做吗？

paragraphs.en: The year 2021 was the first time in human history where a person could say “We are the first generation who won’t die” and not be ridiculed. I saw that and I thought, this is a moment when it comes together, where you see it and you can do something about it.
paragraphs.zh: 2021年是人类历史上第一次，有人可以说“我们是第一代不会死去的一代”，而不会被人嘲笑。我看到了这一点，我想，这是一个所有线索汇聚起来的时刻——你能看见它，而且可以对它采取行动。

paragraphs.en: If you talk to people who know me, if you ask my husband, poor guy, they would say that I’m a very controlled person. I wake up at the same time every day. I do the same exercise. I tend to eat a lot of very similar foods. There’s a lot of routine and structure to my life. When I hear about the way you live, I, as a very controlled person, am astounded at how controlled your life is. Was control always something that was a defining characteristic?
paragraphs.zh: 如果你和认识我的人交谈，如果你问我丈夫，那可怜的家伙，他们会说我这是个非常自律的人。 我每天同一时间起床。会做同样的运动。我的饮食也通常是许多非常相似的食物。我的生活有大量的例行程序和结构。当我听到你的生活方式时，作为一个非常有控制力的人，我对你的生活有如此强的可控性感到震惊。控制力一直是你最鲜明的特征吗？

paragraphs.en: I would reframe it and say you’re actually a really smart engineer; you realize that the metabolic cost of you having to make these decisions every single day is so expensive that you say, “You know what? It’s not worth it. I’m just going to systematize this so that my brain can be allocated toward other higher-level thinking.” I view it the same way: Why would I fight these daily, miscellaneous, ultimately irrelevant decisions on a moment-to-moment basis when they can just be automated? I’d rather spend my scarce brain capacity thinking of higher-level things—about the future of the human race, for example.
paragraphs.zh: 我会重新表述一下：你其实是一个非常聪明的工程师；你意识到自己每天要做这些决策的代谢成本太高，于是会说：“你知道吗？这不值得。我还是把它系统化一下吧，让我的大脑可以分配去做其他更高层次的思考。”我也是这么想的：既然这些日常、杂乱、最终并不关键的当下决策可以被自动化，为什么还要在每个瞬间都去和它们较劲？我宁愿把珍贵的脑力容量留给更高层面的事情——比如人类未来这种更大的议题。

paragraphs.en: In 2023 I was leading the newsroom at Vice, and we published a story on you. You talked about your body image from decades ago. You talked about how hard it was at that time to control yourself around food at night. You said you now often go to bed hungry, and you’ve learned to find joy in that. You eat mostly plants. If I stripped your name out and looked at a bulleted list of those comments, that sounds a lot like me 20 years ago with a very serious eating disorder. It wasn’t about the number on the scale. It wasn’t about how I looked. It was about being able to systematize and control my environment. I think if you were to ask some doctors and psychiatrists, they would say, “Well, that sounds like disordered behavior. That sounds like an eating disorder.” How do you respond to that? Particularly when we’re talking about you as a public figure advocating for a certain approach to health, do you have concerns about what you’re advocating for?
paragraphs.zh: 2023年我在 Vice 负责新闻编辑部，我们刊登过一篇关于你的报道。你谈到了你几十年前的体型观念。你讲到当时晚上在食物面前很难克制自己。你说如今你常常带着饥饿感上床，并且已经学会从中找到快乐。你主要以植物性食物为主。如果我把你的名字去掉，只把这些说法做成要点列表，它会听起来很像二十年前的我，那时我有着非常严重的进食障碍。那并不是关于体重数字，也不是关于我外表。那是关于能够把自己的环境系统化并加以控制。我想，如果你问一些医生和精神科医生，他们可能会说：‘嗯，这听起来像是失调的行为，听起来像进食障碍。’你会如何回应？尤其当我们谈到你作为公共人物时，你在公开倡导一种特定的健康路径，你是否担心你所倡导的东西有问题？

paragraphs.en: Most people I know in America have an eating disorder. I rarely meet somebody who doesn’t find themselves, late in the evening, powerless to stop themselves from eating the ice cream or the cookies or the chips. I clearly struggled with controlling my food intake. I would say it’s really more of a widespread societal problem, that we have an addiction problem with food, our phones, and our entertainment, our scrolling. I don’t know if my addiction is more excessive than other people’s addictions or if it’s just weighted more toward food versus the phone, but I’d say I’m probably pretty average on a population-level addiction scale.
paragraphs.zh: 我在美国认识的大多数人都有进食障碍。我几乎很少见到有人到晚饭后或晚间不会发现自己在控制不住自己——停不下来地吃冰淇淋、曲奇饼或者薯片。我明显曾经也很难控制自己摄入食物。我想它更像一个更广泛的社会问题：我们有一种对食物、对手机、对娱乐、对刷屏行为的成瘾问题。我不知道我的成瘾是否比别人的更严重，或者只是更偏向食物而不是手机，但我会说，在群体层面上，我对成瘾程度可能算是比较平均的。

paragraphs.en: So you’ve taken what used to manifest in one way, which was an inability to control what you were eating and when you were eating it, and essentially adopted a new approach, which is saying, “I eat these things at these times, and I eliminate the variables. I take the choice out of it, and that is how I am going to manage this part of my health.”
paragraphs.zh: 所以你把原本以一种方式表现出来的情况——即无法控制自己吃什么、什么时候吃——转化成了一种新的处理方式，也就是说：‘我在这些时间点吃这些东西，并且把变量去掉。我把选择权移出其中，以这种方式来管理我健康的这一部分。’

paragraphs.en: Exactly. And I guess more broadly it’s a cultural commentary, where I’m saying the most powerful forces in all of society—corporations—are pointing their power at getting you to be addicted to their thing. Whether it’s scrolling their app, eating their food, watching their show—all their intelligence is pointed at you and trying to get you to be addicted.
paragraphs.zh: 没错。我想更宽泛地说，这是一个文化评论：我在指出，社会中最有权势的力量——企业——把权力都用在让你对他们的产品上瘾上。无论是刷他们的应用、吃他们的食物，还是看他们的节目——他们的全部算计都指向你，目标就是让你上瘾。

paragraphs.en: I don’t want to be addicted to anything. I want to have agency and freedom over my existence as much as possible, so that’s what I’m trying to build. I realize that people on the outside looking in, they say, “He’s working through his childhood trauma. He’s working through a food addiction.” I’m open to all those explanations. I would be the first person to be self-deprecating and be like, “This guy has got issues.” Unquestionably.
paragraphs.zh: 我不想对任何东西上瘾。我希望在我自己的存在上拥有尽可能多的主导权和自由，这正是我想要建立的。我要说实话，旁观的人会说：‘他在处理童年创伤。’‘他在处理食物成瘾。’我对这些解释都持开放态度。我会第一个自嘲地说：‘这家伙确实有问题。’这完全无法否认。

paragraphs.en: You’re addicted to longevity.
paragraphs.zh: 你对的是“长寿”上瘾。

paragraphs.en: I mean, there’s probably no way of actually getting over addiction other than controlling where your addiction is pointed.
paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，除了把你的上瘾指向的目标控制住之外，几乎没有真正摆脱上瘾的方法。

paragraphs.en: I want to ask you about MAHA. When President Trump won the election, you congratulated him on social media. You were photographed with RFK Jr. What is your assessment of RFK Jr. and the Trump administration vis-à-vis American health? How do you rate the administration so far?
paragraphs.zh: 我想问你关于 MAHA 的问题。当特朗普当选总统后，你在社交媒体上向他表示了祝贺。你还与 RFK Jr. 一起被拍过照。你如何评估 RFK Jr. 和特朗普政府在美国医疗健康方面的表现？到目前为止，你如何评价这个政府？

paragraphs.en: RFK is certainly not a status quo person.
paragraphs.zh: RFK 绝对不是一个维持现状的人。

paragraphs.en: The status quo in the US is not working. If you look at the data around the health of our citizenry, it’s embarrassingly bad. I think we spend 1.8 times our peer countries in health care: $13,000 per person versus $7,000 or so in the other developed countries. We spend more and we get less. Whether RFK is the solution or not, what we’re doing is not working, so I’m open to change, and I’m open to a variety of possibilities. It’s not to say that everything he’s doing is correct, but I do support the idea that we definitely need to change.
paragraphs.zh: 美国的现状并没有起到应有作用。如果你看看我们公民健康相关的数据，情况令人尴尬。我的理解是，美国在人均医疗支出上是其他发达国家的 1.8 倍：每人约 13,000 美元，而其他发达国家大约是 7,000 美元左右。我们花得更多，却得到更少。无论 RFK 是否就是解决方案，目前这种做法确实不起作用，所以我对改变持开放态度，也愿意考虑多种可能。并不是说他所做的一切都正确，但我确实支持我们必须改变这一事实。

paragraphs.en: Do you worry about medical research being delayed by years or decades if it’s curtailed right now?
paragraphs.zh: 你是否担心，如果现在收紧，医学研究会被推迟数年甚至几十年？

paragraphs.en: Oftentimes, when one path is discontinued, everybody thinks it’s an end of something; but actually, that change produces a new path that people didn’t anticipate. So no, I support the creative destruction. I think it could have some positive outcomes. Clearly, there could be some drawbacks. When you break things like this, it goes both ways. It’s not a clean win or loss.
paragraphs.zh: 往往当一条路被取消时，大家都会觉得这代表着某种结束；但实际上，这种变化会带来一条人们原本没有预料到的新道路。所以不，我支持这种“创造性毁灭”。我认为它可能带来一些积极结果。当然，也可能有一些负面影响。像这样打破旧秩序时，结果总是两面的。它不是简单地一边倒地赢或输。

paragraphs.en: I think what would be cool is if we as a country said, “We want to be number one in the entire world for life expectancy.” That is a very clear goal.
paragraphs.zh: 我想，如果我们作为一个国家说：我们要成为全世界平均寿命第一，这会很酷。那是一个非常明确的目标。

paragraphs.en: Let’s talk about faith, because for so many people, religion or faith is fundamental in informing how they think about life and death. They think about the afterlife, they think about heaven or hell or whatever version of an afterlife exists. If you are not a person of faith, and I am not, you think, “Well, my body disintegrates and goes into the ground and then it helps the trees grow and that’s just how this works. I am a biological organism.” How do you contemplate the idea of an afterlife or what happens after we die, given that you spend a lot of time focused on not dying?
paragraphs.zh: 我们来谈谈信仰，因为对许多人来说，宗教或信仰是他们如何理解生与死的根本。人们会思考来世，会想到天堂、地狱，或者任何一种存在的来世版本。如果你不是一个有信仰的人，而我也不是，你会想：‘我的身体会分解、归于尘土，最终有助于树木生长，这就是这个世界的运作方式。我是一个生物体。’那么，当你把大把时间都花在“如何不死”上时，怎么去思考死后世界，或者说我们死后会发生什么呢？

paragraphs.en: This is the biggest question. This is the one that everybody has to grapple with, which is, What is existence? But for the history of humanity we have not had an opening to ask the question, What does existence mean? Right now is the first opening in literally tens or hundreds of thousands of years. The new answer to existence is that existence itself is the highest virtue.
paragraphs.zh: 这是最大的问题。这是每个人都得面对的问题，也就是：什么是存在？但在人类历史上，我们并没有机会去问：存在意味着什么？而如今，这才是自字面意义上的数万或数十万年以来第一次开端。对存在的全新回答是：存在本身就是最高的德行。

paragraphs.en: Sounds like you now have a religion.
paragraphs.zh: 听起来你现在已经有了一种“信仰”。

paragraphs.en: When did that become an idea, and ultimately … why? You’ve got your public persona, you have your protocol, you have a business. Why bring religion into this?
paragraphs.zh: 这一点又是何时变成了一个观念，最终又是为什么呢？你有公开形象，有你的方法论，有一家企业。为什么要把宗教搬进来？

paragraphs.en: Companies come and go. Countries come and go. Religions have endured for millennia. Confucius built a system of ethics. Muhammad received visions. Jesus was the son of God. Adam Smith wrote about the invisible hand. Karl Marx wrote Das Kapital. The American founding fathers wrote a constitution. Satoshi dropped the White Paper.
paragraphs.zh: 公司会起起落落。国家也会兴衰。宗教却能延续数千年。孔子建立了一套伦理体系。穆罕默德曾得见天启。耶稣是上帝之子。亚当·斯密写下了“看不见的手”。卡尔·马克思写了《资本论》。美国开国元勋们撰写了宪法。中本聪发布了《白皮书》。

paragraphs.en: If you look at the major ideological and technological phenomena of the world, they’ve landed in different ways. It’s very obvious that right now, in this age of AI, a new ideology is going to drop. It always drops in answer to technological disruption. An ideology must help humans make sense of the world. When I look at the world right now, I can’t see any ideology that helps explain this moment, that helps me make practical day-to-day decisions. You can’t go to Christianity and say, “Tell me how I behave in this moment.” You can’t even go to democracy. You can’t go to capitalism.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你观察世界上重大的意识形态和技术现象，会发现它们各自落地的方式不同。很明显，现在正处在人工智能时代，一种新的意识形态即将出现。意识形态总是在技术冲击的背景下产生。它必须帮助人类理解这个世界。当我看当下这个世界时，我看不到任何一种意识形态能解释这个时代，也能帮助我在日常生活中做出实用的决策。你不能去问基督教：“告诉我现在该怎么行动。”你甚至不能去问民主主义。你也不能去问资本主义。

paragraphs.en: I want to make sure I understand. Your premise here is that AI will be this transformational technology that will extend our lifespan in some way, and there is not an ideology that supports mankind at that moment?
paragraphs.zh: 我想确认一下。我在这里的理解是，AI会成为一种以某种方式延长人类寿命的变革性技术，而此时并没有某种意识形态能够站在全人类一边，对吗？

paragraphs.en: I’m saying nobody knows what’s going to happen. All we know is that AI is improving at a rate that is unfathomable to our minds. Now, that doesn’t stop humans from saying, “I will explain to you what’s going to happen. It’s going to make the world an abundant place, and we humans are going to be able to do whatever we want, and we’re going to have one day a week where we work.”
paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是没人知道接下来会发生什么。我们只知道AI正在以一种超出人类想象范围的速度提升。现在，这并不能阻止有人说："我来告诉你们会发生什么。它将让世界成为一个富足的地方，我们人类将能够做想做的任何事，而且我们每周只需要工作一天。"

paragraphs.en: To be clear, I think that’s a very utopian and very unlikely scenario.
paragraphs.zh: 说得明确些，我认为那是一个非常乌托邦、也非常不太可能发生的情景。

paragraphs.en: I agree. I don’t believe any human knows what’s going to happen. I only believe we have an event horizon. Nobody can see past it. This thing is moving very fast. We as a species don’t move that fast. We can’t move that fast. We can’t change our society at that speed. So there’s going to be some kind of dislocation. And I’m trying to say we need to prepare ourselves in the most basic way we can.
paragraphs.zh: 我同意。我不认为任何人知道将来会发生什么。我只认为我们正接近一个事件视界，没有人能看穿它。这个东西正在非常快地变化。我们这个物种本身就不以这种速度前进。我们不可能那么快改变。我们也不能以那样的速度改变社会。因此，一定会出现某种程度的错位。我想表达的就是：我们需要以最基本的方式做好准备。

paragraphs.en: My solution for this is we choose to not die. That’s it. I’m not arguing for immortality. I’m not arguing for utopia. We as a species, our existence is at risk. We do not know if humans have a role in the future. We do not know if we’re going to survive this moment. We are already at each other’s throats. We have nuclear annihilation as a possibility. It’s a moment where we evolve into a species who say: The single thing we have in common is that nobody wants to die right now. That’s it.
paragraphs.zh: 我的解决方案是我们选择不死。就是这样。我并不是在主张不朽。我也不是在主张乌托邦。作为一个物种，我们的存在正处于危险之中。我们不知道人类在未来是否有立足之地。我们不知道自己是否会度过此刻。我们彼此之间已经到了咬牙切齿的地步。核毁灭已经成为一种可能。现在这是一个时刻——我们将进化成这样一个物种：我们共同拥有的唯一一点是，没人现在想死。就是这样。

paragraphs.en: What is required for someone to be a disciple of this religion?
paragraphs.zh: 要成为这种“宗教”的追随者，需要什么？

paragraphs.en: At a foundational level, it’s a humility to say we don’t know what’s coming. Therefore, the most sober and practical thing to do is lock in our individual and shared behaviors. I’m not going to do things that kill myself. We’re not going to kill each other. We don’t kill the planet.
paragraphs.zh: 在根本层面上，这是一种谦逊——承认我们不知道未来会怎样。因此，最清醒、最现实的做法就是约束个人和共同体的行为。我不会做会让我死亡的事情。我们也不会互相残杀。我们不会杀死地球。

paragraphs.en: And then we align AI with Don’t Die. Right now you’ve got the US developing AI, and China developing AI, and open-source models, and closed-source models, and we’re basically just putting it all into this coliseum where everyone’s going to fight it out for power. I don’t think we want to give birth to AI in a warlike scenario.
paragraphs.zh: 然后，我们要把人工智能与“Don’t Die”统一起来。现在美国在发展 AI，中国也在发展 AI，有开源模型，也有闭源模型，我们基本上是在把这些全部放进一座竞技场，让每个人都在其中为权力进行厮杀。我认为我们不想在战争般的局面下诞生 AI。

paragraphs.en: So wouldn’t aligning your movement, in the context of AI, have more to do with making sure the AIs don’t kill us than taking supplements? You’re saying that so much of what you do is not about health, but so much of what you put into the world is about health.
paragraphs.zh: 那么，在人工智能的语境下，让你的行动与现实方向一致，不应该更多地是为了确保AI不会杀掉我们吗，而不是为了服用补充剂？你说得没错：你所做的大部分事情并不是“关于健康”，但你投入世界的许多内容确实是关于健康的。

paragraphs.en: I do feel like this is legitimately an opening that we haven’t seen for a few thousand years, for a new global ideology to emerge rapidly and be the fastest-growing ideology in history. Something’s going to rise and fill this void, whether it’s Don’t Die or something else. So I was trying to figure out, how do you actually talk about this? People don’t care about philosophy. They don’t really care about ideas, not until it’s really important. What they do care about is their health, how they feel in the morning, how they look. I tried to approach this conversation in a way that would be understandable, where Don’t Die as a philosophy is going to bed on time and eating nutritious foods and saying no to junk food. Once you get people in, and they can understand health is a really good thing, you can bridge to philosophy and be like, “There’s this bigger thing going on that we can talk about.”
paragraphs.zh: 我确实觉得这是一个几千年来从未出现过的局面，让一种新的全球意识形态迅速兴起，并成为历史上增长最快的意识形态。无论是“不要死”（Don’t Die）还是别的什么，都将出现并填补这个空白。所以我一直在想，实际上该如何谈论这个问题？人们并不关心哲学。他们真的不太在意理念，至少在觉得它足够重要之前不是这样。人们真正关心的是自己的健康，早上醒来时的状态，自己的外表。我试着用一种大家能理解的方式来展开这场对话：把“Don’t Die”这一哲学先讲成按时睡觉、吃营养食物、拒绝垃圾食品。只要你先让人们进来，并且他们能理解健康确实很好，你就可以进一步过渡到更高层的哲学讨论，对他们说：‘其实还有一件更大的事情值得我们讨论。’

paragraphs.en: So you are the founder of a religion. You are also the CEO of Blueprint; you sell supplements and a variety of products. You sell a finger pinprick aging test. Where do the religion and the commercial business start and stop? Where do they overlap? And why run a commercial enterprise off the back of this?
paragraphs.zh: 所以你既是一个宗教创始人，也是 Blueprint 的 CEO；你出售补充剂和各类产品。你还在出售一种指尖采血的衰老检测。那宗教和商业行为从哪里开始、在哪里结束？它们在哪里重叠？为什么要在这股浪潮上经营一家商业企业？

paragraphs.en: I agree with you a hundred percent. And honestly, I am so close to either shutting it down or selling it.
paragraphs.zh: 我完全同意你的观点。说实话，我几乎已经到了要么关闭它、要么把它出售的边缘。

paragraphs.en: I’ve been talking to people about this. I don’t need the money, and it’s a pain-in-the-ass company. Practically, I was having to solve these basic problems myself, like how do I find clean protein powder that is tested by a third-party lab and has low heavy metals? I need it for my body. Once I started doing it, friends were like, “Can I have some?” I’m like, “Sure.” It just evolved in a way where I was trying to do people a solid. The problem is now people see the business and give me less credibility on the philosophy side. I will not make that trade-off. It is not worth it to me. So yeah, I don’t want it.
paragraphs.zh: 我一直在和人讨论这件事。我不需要这笔钱，而且这是一家烦人的公司。实际上，我得自己去解决这些基本问题，比如：我怎么找到经过第三方实验室检测、并且重金属含量很低的干净蛋白粉？我身体需要它。等我开始自己做这件事后，朋友们就会说：“我也能用一些吗？”我会说：“可以。”这件事就这样发展了，我本来是想帮朋友们一把。问题是，现在人们看到这家公司，就会让我在理念这一面显得没那么可信。我不会做这种权衡。对我来说这不值得。所以，没错，我不想要它。

paragraphs.en: The New York Times published a story about you recently, and that story included reporting about the integrity of the products that you sell and about the financial health of the company. Is the decision to potentially sell or shut the company down informed by that, either that controversy or the fact that the company isn’t working the way you had hoped?
paragraphs.zh: 你最近被《纽约时报》报道过，那篇文章提到了你出售的产品的真实性，以及公司的财务状况。这次可能出售或关闭公司的决定，是否受到这件事影响——不论是那场争议，还是公司没有按你预期运作？

paragraphs.en: It has nothing to do with The New York Times. I am not hiding from the New York Times article. I’m happy to take, head-on, every single allegation they made. I will say [their reporting on] the business, that was fucking made up. They take things and they contort it to fit their narrative, but anybody who’s been on the side of a hit piece knows it’s bullshit. You know it. I know it. [“We are confident about the accuracy of our story,” says New York Times spokesperson Danielle Rhoades Ha, “which included reviewing legal and internal documents as well as interviewing 30 people close to Mr. Johnson and his company.” —Ed.]
paragraphs.zh: 这与《纽约时报》毫无关系。我并没有躲着那篇《纽约时报》的文章。我愿意正面回应他们提的每一条指控。我得说一句：[关于这家公司的报道，完全是胡编乱造。他们抓住一些东西然后扭曲去迎合他们的叙事，但任何见过钓鱼式抹黑文章的人都知道这就是废话。你知道这点，我也知道。] [“我们对报道准确性有信心，”《纽约时报》发言人丹妮埃尔·罗伊兹·哈（Danielle Rhoades Ha）表示，“我们审阅了法律和内部文件，并采访了离约翰逊先生及其公司较近的30位人士。”——Ed.]

paragraphs.en: As a journalist, I believe that our industry operates in good faith. I respect The New York Times. I read them every day. I know a lot of great journalists who work there. Obviously I had nothing to do with reporting that piece. Is the company right now a profitable company? Are you running a profitable company, whether you sell it or disband it?
paragraphs.zh: 作为一名记者，我相信我们这个行业是出于善意运作的。我尊重《纽约时报》。我每天都看他们，我认识很多在那里做得非常出色的记者。显然，这篇报道的采写与我无关。现在这家公司是有盈利吗？不管你是打算卖掉它，还是把它解散，你现在是在经营一家盈利的公司吗？

paragraphs.en: They painted it like we are in some kind of emergency financial situation. That is not the case. We are break-even, and I’ve said that publicly many times. We’ve had profitable months, we’ve had loss months. I’ve been very clear, we priced our products at the exact level to basically be break-even. Additional margin is just not worth it to me. It’s been a consistent strategy the entire time.
paragraphs.zh: 他们把这件事说得像我们处在某种紧急的财务危机中，但事实并非如此。我们是收支持平的，我公开说过很多次。我们有过盈利的月份，也有过亏损的月份。我一直说得很清楚：我们的产品定价基本上就是为了做到收支平衡。对我来说，追求额外利润并不值得。这个策略从头到尾都很一致。

paragraphs.en: The thrust of the New York Times piece was around how you use confidentiality agreements. You have a former fiancée who was also a former employee, and at least two other former employees, who have filed complaints with the National Labor Relations Board about confidentiality agreements that sound very extensive. In some cases, they’re 20-plus pages. Sometimes there’s an additional opt-in agreement.
paragraphs.zh: 《纽约时报》文章的核心在于你如何使用保密协议。你有一位前未婚妻，同时也是前雇员，还有至少另外两位前雇员，他们向国家劳工关系委员会提出了关于保密协议的投诉，这些协议听起来非常严格。在某些情况下，协议长度超过二十页。有时还会再加一份附加的自愿加入协议。

paragraphs.en: You’re so transparent about yourself publicly. I know so much about your body—more than I would like to—and that’s my choice. I can close the tab if I don’t want to hear more about your penis. But you are so transparent, and then you’re essentially saying to your employees, “Support me in my transparency, but don’t fucking say anything yourself,” right?
paragraphs.zh: 你对自己在公开场合非常透明。我对你的身体了解得太多——比我愿意知道的还要多——而这是我自己的选择。要是我不想再听到你那方面的细节，我可以关闭页面。但是你确实很坦率，然后你又基本上是在对员工说：‘支持我这种透明，却别他妈自己说一句’。对吗？

paragraphs.en: I grew up poor. My mom made my clothes for me. I was poor until I was 34, and then I made a couple hundred million dollars. I’m new money. In that moment, when I made that money, I did not understand what it means to have money and how it fundamentally changes your relationship with the entire world.
paragraphs.zh: 我是在贫穷中长大的。我的妈妈亲手给我做衣服。直到34岁之前我都很穷，而后我赚到了数亿美元。我是“新钱”。那一刻，当我赚到那笔钱时，我还不明白有了钱意味着什么，也不明白它如何从根本上改变你与整个世界的关系。

paragraphs.en: Over the past 12 years, I’ve learned what it means to have money. I will tell you, and anyone who’s been in this situation will tell you the same, it changes everything. My fiancée attempted to extract $9 million from me, and she used, quote-unquote, “the most feared law firm” in the whole world. They sent me this really scary 13-page letter and said, “Look, we’re going to say these terrible things about you, but you can make it all go away if you just pay us $9 million this week.” It was the first legitimate attempt on [my money]. [Quinn Emanuel, the law firm, declined to comment. In its settlement offer, it proposed that Southern would “enter into a full mutual release of all claims” against Johnson and “give up rights to use her life experiences” with him for the flat sum. —Ed.]
paragraphs.zh: 过去12年里，我学会了金钱意味着什么。我可以告诉你，也会有过这种经历的人都这么说：它能改变一切。我的未婚妻试图向我榨取900万美元，并且她动用了整个世界上所谓“最令人闻风丧胆的”律师事务所。他们给我寄来了一封非常吓人的13页信，里面说："听着，我们会把这些可怕的话说出来，但你只要在本周内给我们支付900万美元，就能把这一切都让它消失。" 这是对[我的钱]的第一次正式施压。[其律师事务所奎恩·埃马纽EL（Quinn Emanuel）拒绝置评。在其和解提案中，南方被要求“与约翰逊达成全部诉讼请求的完全互相豁免”，并“放弃其与他共同生活经历中使用其人生经历”所获得的任何权利，以换取一笔固定金额。—编者注]

paragraphs.en: This is at the tail end of “Me Too,” where I had friends and others losing their entire professional life over something, right? So it was a very high-risk situation for many people, who were seeing very serious consequences for public allegations.
paragraphs.zh: 这是“#MeToo”运动尾声时的一幕，当时我身边有朋友和其他人因为某件事而失去了整个职业生涯，对吧？因此这对许多人来说是高风险局面，因为大家都在看到公开指控带来的非常严重的后果。

paragraphs.en: Often deservedly so.
paragraphs.zh: 这其中往往有应有的成分。

paragraphs.en: Sure. Whether deserved or not, when a phenomenon like that emerges, a cottage industry will emerge and people will say, “This is an opportunistic time for me.” We know there’s a variety of legitimate and not-legitimate situations. Do I cave and pay $9 million to make this go away quietly, or do I stand my ground? I slept on it and said, “I’m going to stand my ground. What they’re saying is not true, and I’m not going to buckle. I’m not going to pay for this.”
paragraphs.zh: 当然。无论事情是否应如此，当这类现象出现时，就会出现一种“顺势谋利”的小产业，人们会说："这是我能捞一把的机会。" 我们知道其中既有合理的情况，也有不合理的情况。我该屈服，支付900万美元让风波悄悄过去，还是坚持到底？我想了两天，决定说："我会坚持。因为他们说的都不属实，我不会屈服。我不会为这件事付费。"

paragraphs.en: To be clear, the allegations in the letter, in your view, were false?
paragraphs.zh: 先说清楚，按你的看法，那封信里的指控是不实的吗？

paragraphs.en: After two years of legal disputes, if you read the report, the arbitrator and then the superior court judge said there was no evidence for what she said. That whole legal process vindicated me, after millions of dollars in legal fees. Five years later, it’s resurfaced through The New York Times, and they don’t mention any of that. They don’t mention that she was discredited as a truth-telling person through this legal process, that she’s on record having said things that are just fundamentally not true. [LTL Attorneys, which represented Southern in her 2021 lawsuit against Johnson, declined to comment on litigation. According to court documents, the arbitrator did not adjudicate Southern’s claims, as she found that Southern “had not raised a triable issue.” Similarly, the state judge ruled Southern had “not established grounds to vacate the arbitration award,” which she had contested. —Ed.]
paragraphs.zh: 两年诉讼打下来后，你看那份报告的时候，仲裁员以及随后的一位上诉法院法官都说了，没有任何证据支持她的说法。整整一场法律程序都在为我作证，尽管我花了数百万美元的律师费。五年之后，它又借《纽约时报》重现出来，但他们没有提及任何这些。他们没有提到，她在这场法律程序中作为证人的可信度被推翻，事实是她曾公开说过一些从根本上就是不真实的内容。[LTL 律师事务所曾代表 Southern 在她 2021 年对 Johnson 提起诉讼，但拒绝就该案诉讼发表评论。根据法庭文件，仲裁员未对 Southern 的主张作出裁决，因为她未能“提出可供审理的问题”。同样，州法官裁定 Southern“未能证明废除仲裁裁决的理由”，而这也是她所质疑的内容。——编辑注]

paragraphs.en: When I’m walking into this world of Blueprint, I’m saying, “OK, now I’ve seen a few patterns here where people use things as an advantage for their own gain, and sometimes it ends up in an extortion-like effort.” So I said, “To address this, I’m going to be incredibly transparent. I’m going to say: When you come and work in my environment, this is what you could expect.” It was the most fair gesture possible.
paragraphs.zh: 当我进入 Blueprint 的这个世界时，我是在说：‘好吧，我已经看到一些模式了，有些人会把事情当作谋取私利的筹码，有时这演变成了一种敲诈式行为。’ 所以我说：‘为了解决这个问题，我会做到极度透明。我会说：你如果愿意来到我的环境工作，你就可以预期会是这样的。’ 这才是最公平的做法。

paragraphs.en: I understand this idea that you amass wealth and people see opportunity in that. And that if you want to come work at a company where someone is talking about their erections on the internet, they should sign something testifying that they are OK with that. What I’m not quite clear on is placing intensive limitations and restrictions on what employees can and cannot say about their work environment and their company. How does that tie into what you’re describing?
paragraphs.zh: 我明白这样的观念：你积累了财富，于是别人会看到机会。再者，如果你想去一家某个人在网上公开谈论自己性能力的公司工作，那么他们应该先签署一份文件，表明自己对此没意见。我不太明白的是，为什么要对员工在其工作环境和公司方面能说什么、不能说什么设置如此严格的限制和禁令。那与你所描述的做法到底如何衔接？

paragraphs.en: I think you would agree that that is standard practice for a corporation. When you are a corporate entity, and even when you’re a married couple, there’s things you discuss in private which you don’t discuss in public. Corporations have boundary conditions around information, whether it’s IP, whether it’s product development. But everybody has rules and systems for how you control information. Almost nobody in society operates with complete transparency, because everybody has learned life lessons that things go wrong. People will use this to their advantage and to other people’s detriment. What I’m doing is not atypical at all.
paragraphs.zh: 我想你会同意，这对一家公司来说是标准做法。即使当你是一家企业，或者你还是一对夫妻，总有一些东西你会在私下讨论，却不会在公众场合谈起。公司会为信息设置边界条件，不管是知识产权，也不管是产品开发方面的内容。但在社会里，几乎每个人都有自己的信息控制规则和体系。几乎没有人能做到完全透明，因为每个人都从生活经验中学到：事情可能会出错。人们会利用这一点来为自己谋利，并且对他人不利地加以利用。我所做的并不是什么反常之举。

paragraphs.en: In your view, do you run a safe and supportive workplace?
paragraphs.zh: 在你看来，你是在营造一个安全且有支持性的工作环境吗？

paragraphs.en: Is this a good place to work?
paragraphs.zh: 这是一个适合工作的好地方吗？

paragraphs.en: You feel very confident in that?
paragraphs.zh: 你对此有足够的自信吗？

paragraphs.en: We have three goals at the company. The first goal is that our customers write us love letters. That’s the most important goal. Number two is that people say it’s the best job they’ve ever had. And three is that we say we are the best in the industry at what we do. I say this every single week in our company meetings. And on number two, I’ll say, “What this means, you guys, is if you’ve got an issue that you’re dealing with, if something is causing you to not like your circumstance, if it’s a process that’s annoying, if it’s something that doesn’t make sense to you, if your screen is too small, whatever the issue is, you have the opportunity to raise it. And when you raise it, we’re going to show you we have the organizational capacity to address it.”
paragraphs.zh: 我们在公司有三项目标。第一个目标是让客户给我们写情书。这是最重要的目标。第二个是让人们说这是他们做过的最好的一份工作。第三个是让大家说我们在所做的事情上是行业内最强的。我每周都在公司会议里说这句话。说到第二个目标时，我会补充：你们也要明白，这意味着如果你遇到一个问题，如果有事让你不满意，如果某个流程让你觉得烦，或者有什么事情让你不理解，如果你的屏幕太小，不管是什么问题，你都有机会把它提出来。你一提出来，我们就会向你证明我们有组织能力去处理。

paragraphs.en: Most of the time when people experience things that they’re not happy about, the course of action is to talk to the coworker and be like, “I’m going to gossip about these things.” I don’t like it. I call this pebbles in the shoe.
paragraphs.zh: 大多数时候，当人们遇到自己不开心的事，做法通常是去和同事交谈，然后开始“把这些事悄悄聊开”。我不喜欢这样。我把它称作“鞋里的小石子”。

paragraphs.en: Do you have an HR person who helps with the pebbles?
paragraphs.zh: 你们有专门的 HR 人员来帮忙处理这些“小石子”吗？

paragraphs.en: Yes. We call this person out and we say, for any issue you have, you can go directly to her; anything you have, you can raise it.
paragraphs.zh: 有。我们会把这个人点出来，并且告诉大家：无论你有什么问题，都可以直接去找她；你有什么都可以提出。

paragraphs.en: Do you like being famous? From when we assigned that story at Vice in 2023 to now, you have become much more famous. I think the more famous someone becomes, to some degree, the less they can control the narrative out there about them.
paragraphs.zh: 你喜欢出名吗？从2023年我们在Vice给你安排那篇报道到现在，你变得更有名了很多。我认为，一个人越出名，在某种程度上，他越难完全控制外界对他的叙事。

paragraphs.en: Do you worry about a level of fame where you can’t systematically address the entire world and what’s being said? In the Netflix documentary, you seemed to take great joy and almost glee in reading nasty shit people say about you on the internet.
paragraphs.zh: 你会担心那种无法系统性回应全世界、无法一一回应所有说法的名声吗？在Netflix纪录片里，你似乎对那些网民对你说的恶毒言论既享受又几乎感到兴奋。

paragraphs.en: I’ll take all the dunks about me all day long. It’s when they say something about you that challenges your trustworthiness—that’s what I would isolate. It’s trust that I deeply care about, and being respected by the 25th century. Maybe humans are not around, but whatever form of intelligence is around, I really care about them saying, “You know what? We’re grateful that there was a guy who tried to piece this thing together.” If my trust is in question, I can’t go after these bigger goals.
paragraphs.zh: 我可以整天接住大家对我骂的任何一击。真正让我在意的是：当他们说出某件事情，去挑战你的可信度——那才是我会特别在意的地方。我非常看重的是信任，以及被25世纪的人尊重。也许人类不在了，但无论哪一种智慧形态存在，我都真心希望他们会说：‘你知道吗？我们很感激，曾经有一个人试图把这件事拼凑起来。’如果我的可信度受到质疑，我就无法去追求那些更宏大的目标。

paragraphs.en: On fame, I think it’s fantastic. If I had to choose between fame and a billion dollars, I would choose fame 100 times out of 100. It’s very hard to achieve. It’s uniquely valuable. I get access to almost anyone in the entire world at this point. If the goal is to create the fastest-growing ideology in the history of the human race and to pair it with the time when the species is evolving into something else, you need fame.
paragraphs.zh: 谈到名声，我认为这太好了。如果让我在名声和十亿美元之间做选择，我会一百次都选名声。它非常难以获得，而且是独一无二的价值。我现在几乎可以接触到世界上几乎任何一个人。如果目标是在人类历史上创造增长速度最快的意识形态，并且把它与人类物种正在进化成另一种形态的时代相结合，那么你就需要名声。

paragraphs.en: So you’re not thinking about your legacy in a 10-year, 20-year, 50-year timeline, you’re thinking about hundreds of years?
paragraphs.zh: 也就是说，你不是在按10年、20年、50年的时间线思考自己的遗产，而是把视角放在几百年的范围里？

paragraphs.en: It’s the only time that makes sense to me. I did a thought experiment where I imagined being present in the 25th century. I’m sitting with them, and they’re talking in whatever form they’re talking, and they are looking back on the early 21st century. Just like we look back at other centuries and we compress them into a few major things but otherwise we don’t get into the details, they will do the same thing for the 21st century.
paragraphs.zh: 这对我来说才是唯一合理的时间尺度。我做过一次思想实验，设想自己处在25世纪。我和他们坐在一起，他们用着自己那一代人使用的某种沟通方式，回望21世纪初。就像我们回看过去的其他世纪，会把它们压缩成几个关键事件，但通常不会深入到细节一样，他们也会对21世纪做同样的处理。

paragraphs.en: So if they do that, what do they say? I thought about this question for years. One: They would say that’s when humanity gave birth to superintelligence. Two: That’s when humanity figured out that they were the first generation who wouldn’t die. And so I thought, how can you possibly act now on that information? You could build a biotech company, or you could try to pass governmental policy. But it’s very obvious we need a new ideological framework, and what’s more powerful than religion?
paragraphs.zh: 那么如果他们这么做，会怎么说？我思考这个问题很多年。第一：他们会说，那就是人类孕育出超智能的时代。第二：那就是人类意识到自己是第一代不会死亡的一代。于是我想，得知了这些信息之后，你现在还能做什么？你可以建立一家生物技术公司，也可以尝试推动政府政策。但很明显，我们需要一个新的意识形态框架，而什么比宗教更有力量呢？

paragraphs.en: So in the 25th century, when people are sitting somewhere talking about that guy Bryan who did this and that and the other thing, what are you doing?
paragraphs.zh: 所以在25世纪，当人们坐在某个地方聊起那个叫布莱恩的人，聊着他做了这些、做了那些以及其他那些事时，你会在做什么？

paragraphs.en: I joke that I am inevitably going to die from this ironic thing.
paragraphs.zh: 我开玩笑地说，我迟早会因为这件反差感十足的事而死。

paragraphs.en: I mean, that tree right there is about to fall, and it’s going to be over right now.
paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，那里那棵树快要倒下了，而这一切也许就在此刻结束。

paragraphs.en: Exactly. At least you will have been witness to it.
paragraphs.zh: 没错，至少你会亲眼见证这一幕。

paragraphs.en: It would be an amazing story.
paragraphs.zh: 这将会是个惊人的故事。

paragraphs.en: The article would be a banger. That’s the thing: It’d be good for business for you.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章会是一篇爆款内容。问题在于：对你而言，它会带来不错的商业价值。

paragraphs.en: Let us know what you think about this article. Submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh: 请告诉我们你对这篇文章的看法。请向编辑寄信，[email protected]。
